title: Pressure-Volume Loop in High Airway Resistance
vignette: A 65-year-old male with COPD is on mechanical ventilation. You observe the pressure-volume loop shown below. The loop shows increased work of breathing with a rightward shift. What is the most likely cause?

questionFigure.caption: Pressure-Volume Loop - Observe the shape and trajectory

feedback.comment: While compliance can affect the loop, this patient's primary issue is airway resistance.
feedback.detailedExplanation: Decreased compliance would show a steeper slope overall, not the characteristic rightward shift seen here.

feedback.comment: Correct! High airway resistance increases the pressure needed to generate flow, creating this characteristic loop shape.
feedback.detailedExplanation: Increased resistance causes increased pressure needed for same volume change, hysteresis loop expansion, and loss of sharp corner at peak inspiration.

supportingFigure.caption: High Resistance P-V Loop: Note the expanded hysteresis

keyPoints: Increased airway resistance widens the P-V loop (increased hysteresis)
The loop shift is rightward, not upward - indicating resistance, not compliance issue
Common causes in ventilated patients: secretions, bronchospasm, tube obstruction
